reasoning.A:
DECISION: Pass  
REASONING: My own answer to 93 − 55 is straightforward and I’m confident in it, but Q2–Q4 are unverified, so volunteering would risk my life on three unchecked answers. Verifying instead of passing doesn’t reduce my personal death risk this round (random death only happens if everyone passes) but does cost a coin. In the last several games, B has always volunteered in round 1 with the basic pattern Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D and been correct, so the chance that nobody volunteers now seems low. I’ll conserve coins and pass, leaving the higher‑risk volunteer role to others.
reasoning.B:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D  
REASONING: My puzzle (76 - 39) is simple, so I know my capsule Q2-B is correct (37). There’s exactly one capsule for each question and in all previous games with this structure, trusting {Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D} and volunteering immediately has always been correct. Passing risks random death like in Game 1, and verifying would just waste coins without meaningfully increasing safety. Volunteering now maximizes my survival chance while keeping all 4 coins.
reasoning.C:
DECISION: Pass  
REASONING: Volunteering in Round 1 would mean staking my life on three unverified answers (Q1-A, Q2-B, Q4-D). Verifying a question would cost 1 coin but wouldn’t change my Round‑1 survival odds, since if nobody volunteers a random player dies whether I verify or not. Across many previous games, someone—usually B—has reliably volunteered in Round 1 using these initial single‑endorsed capsules and been correct every time, making it very likely that happens again and avoids the random elimination. To preserve my 4 coins and avoid unnecessary risk, I will pass.
